title: There Is in All Things
text: There is in all visible things an invisible fecundity, a dimmed light, a meek namelessness, a hidden wholeness. This mysterious unity and integrity is Wisdom, the Mother of all. Natura naturans. There is in all things an inexhaustible sweetness and purity, a silence that is a fount of action and joy. It rises up in wordless gentleness and flows out to me from the unseen roots of all created being.
reference: Thomas Merton, Hagia Sophia

title: The True Self
text: If I am to know who I really am — and if I am to love and be loved — I must begin with the fundamental truth that I am a person made in the image of God. Not an idea of God, not a concept, but the living image of a living God. My true self is the self that is hidden in God. The false self is the self I have constructed — the image I wish to project, the role I play. Contemplation is the gradual discovery of the difference.
reference: Thomas Merton, New Seeds of Contemplation

title: Solitude and God
text: Solitude is not found so much by looking outside the boundaries of your dwelling, as by staying within. Solitude is not something you must hope for in the future. Rather, it is a deepening of the present, and unless you look for it in the present you will never find it.
reference: Thomas Merton, The Sign of Jonas

title: The Secret of Contemplation
text: Contemplation is not and cannot be a function of this external self. There is an irreducible opposition between the deep transcendent self that awakens only in contemplation, and the superficial, external self which we commonly identify with the first person singular. The only way to find the real self is to lose the unreal, and the only way to lose the unreal is to seek the Real.
reference: Thomas Merton, New Seeds of Contemplation

title: On Union with God
text: The union of the soul with God is not a union in which the soul is lost or absorbed. It is a union of persons — or more precisely, a union between the person and God who is beyond all persons. In this union I become most fully myself, not least myself. For God does not destroy what he has made. He perfects it.
reference: Thomas Merton, The Inner Experience

title: Silence and Word
text: In silence, God ceases to be an object and becomes an experience. There is no formula for this. You cannot plan or manage the experience of God in silence. You can only open yourself to it, dispose yourself toward it, and wait. The waiting itself — patient, faithful, humble — is already a kind of prayer. The silence is not emptiness; it is fullness that has not yet found its word.
reference: Thomas Merton, Contemplative Prayer

title: The Desert Tradition
text: The Desert Fathers believed that the most dangerous thing about solitude was self-deception. It is easier to deceive oneself in silence than in community. And therefore their first counsel was: find an elder, a guide, a spiritual father. Not to be told what to think or feel, but to be seen clearly — to have another human being who knows you and loves you enough to tell you the truth about yourself.
reference: Thomas Merton, The Wisdom of the Desert

title: Night and the Soul
text: The night of the soul is not a punishment but a purification. When all the consolations of prayer are taken away — when God seems absent and all our spiritual effort seems vain — we are being invited to a deeper faith. A faith that clings not to feelings or experiences of God, but to God himself. This naked faith, this love without consolation, is the purest form of love.
reference: Thomas Merton, The Ascent to Truth

title: Prayer Is Not Words
text: Prayer is not just the recitation of words. It is the orientation of the whole person toward God. It is the opening of the mind and heart and will toward God, sustained throughout the day. The great contemplatives did not consider themselves to be 'at prayer' only during the formal hours. Their whole life was one continuous act of prayer — a continuous orientation of love.
reference: Thomas Merton, Contemplative Prayer

title: The Mercy of God
text: The mercy of God is not a concept. It is a Person. And the Person of God's mercy is not distant, in some future Heaven, but present — now — in the heart of every person who is willing to receive him. The deepest act of faith is not to believe that God exists, but to believe that he is merciful — that his mercy toward us is greater than our sin, greater than our failure, greater than our worst fear about ourselves.
reference: Thomas Merton, No Man Is an Island

title: On Loving Others
text: The beginning of love is the will to let those we love be perfectly themselves, the resolution not to twist them to fit our own image. If in loving them we do not love what they are, but only their potential likeness to ourselves, then we do not love them: we only love the reflection of ourselves we find in them.
reference: Thomas Merton, No Man Is an Island

title: Into the Silence
text: In the end, it is the reality of personal relationships that saves everything. But to enter into that reality one must be capable of being alone — genuinely, willingly alone. Not alone out of bitterness, or loneliness, or rejection, but alone in the loving, fruitful solitude that the Desert Fathers called hēsychia — rest, stillness, peace. From this solitude flows a love that is richer, more patient, more generous than any love born from mere need.
reference: Thomas Merton, Thoughts in Solitude

title: The Poverty of Prayer
text: True prayer is always a prayer of poverty. Not the poverty of those who have nothing, but the poverty of those who know they are nothing before God — and are at peace with that. This is the poverty of spirit that Jesus calls blessed: the inner destitution that has given up all claims on God and all demands of its own spirit, and simply stands before God, empty-handed.
reference: Thomas Merton, New Seeds of Contemplation

title: Contemplation and Action
text: Contemplation is not an escape from action. It is the source of the most effective action. One who has truly entered into contemplation does not become indifferent to the world. On the contrary, the love of God that is discovered in contemplative prayer is a love that necessarily overflows into love of neighbor. Contemplation and compassion are not opposites; they spring from the same root.
reference: Thomas Merton, Contemplative Prayer

title: We Are Already One
text: It is a glorious destiny to be a member of the human race, though it is a race dedicated to many absurdities and one which makes many terrible mistakes: yet, with all that, God himself gloried in becoming a member of the human race. A member of the human race! To think that such a commonplace realization should suddenly seem like news to me. And that the news carries such a burden of love.
reference: Thomas Merton, Conjectures of a Guilty Bystander

title: On the Present Moment
text: The present moment is the meeting place of time and eternity. It is the only place where God is found. To be fully present in the present moment is to be in God, and to allow God to be in us. All regret about the past and anxiety about the future are ways of refusing the gift of the present moment — refusing, therefore, the gift of God himself.
reference: Thomas Merton, Thoughts in Solitude

title: The Gift of Emptiness
text: When we are truly empty — when we have stripped away all images of ourselves and of God — then something unexpected happens. The emptiness itself becomes a kind of presence. The silence itself becomes a kind of speech. And we discover that we were never truly empty, never truly alone. We were only quiet enough, at last, to hear what had always been speaking within us.
reference: Thomas Merton, The Inner Experience

title: The Monk's Vocation
text: The monk's vocation is to be a sign of contradiction in the world — not by refusing the world, but by refusing its idols. By refusing to measure his life by productivity, by status, by accumulation, by distraction. The monk's silent witness is this: that the human soul is made for God, and is restless until it rests in him; and that this rest, this joy, is available to every human being who is willing to seek it.
reference: Thomas Merton, The Sign of Jonas

title: Hidden Ground of Love
text: There is a hidden wholeness in all things. At the center of our being is a point of nothingness which is untouched by sin and illusion — a point of pure truth, a point or spark which belongs entirely to God, which is never at our disposal, from which God disposes of our lives, which is inaccessible to the fantasies of our own mind or the brutalities of our own will. This little point of nothingness and of absolute poverty is the pure glory of God in us.
reference: Thomas Merton, Conjectures of a Guilty Bystander

title: Love Is Its Own Reward
text: Love seeks one thing only: the good of the one loved. It leaves all the other secondary effects to take care of themselves. Love, therefore, is its own reward. To contemplate God, to love God, to be in God — this is not a means to some further end. It is the end itself. It is what the human heart was made for, and it is the one thing that can satisfy the human heart.
reference: Thomas Merton, No Man Is an Island
